title: Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss
oneLiner: A rapid (<72h) hearing loss of cochlear/retrocochlear origin; an ENT emergency where early steroids salvage hearing, but mimic stroke must be excluded first.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Vertebrobasilar stroke** – the single finding that says "this is not SSNHL": **any neurological symptom** (vertigo with nystagmus, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria, hemiparesis, unexplained hiccups) or **HINTS positive** (head impulse normal, nystagmus direction-changing, test of skew vertical). Do not start steroids until CNS cause ruled out.
- **Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)** – not immediately fatal, but missed diagnosis leads to permanent hearing loss and brainstem compression; red flag: **unilateral hearing loss with ipsilateral facial numbness, tinnitus, or imbalance** – needs MRI IAM/CPA.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** – hearing loss can be the sole presentation of temporal bone infection; findings: **fever, neck stiffness, headache, altered mental status** – perform urgent LP if suspected.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset (<72h) of hearing loss in one ear (rarely bilateral)
  - Often noticed on awakening or while using phone
  - Tinnitus (ringing/buzzing) in affected ear – >80% of patients
  - Aural fullness (like "ear needs popping") – may mimic OME
  - Vertigo/dizziness **only in 30–40%** – if present, strongly suspect stroke or labyrinthitis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Otoscopy:** normal ear canal and tympanic membrane (TM) – no wax, no fluid, no perforation. If TM abnormal → conductive loss, not SSNHL.
  - **Rinne test**: air conduction > bone conduction (Rinne positive) – confirms sensorineural component
  - **Weber test**: lateralizes to **better ear** (if unilateral loss)
  - **Neurological exam**: full cranial nerves, cerebellar tests, gait – **must be normal** to rule out stroke
  - **Vestibular exam (if dizzy)**: head impulse test, nystagmus, test of skew – HINTS exam to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely** → **Idiopathic SSNHL** (70-90%): no identifiable cause after standard workup; diagnosis of exclusion.
- **Meniere’s disease**: episodic vertigo + fluctuating hearing + fullness + tinnitus; differentiate by **episodic vertigo lasting hours**, not just at onset.
- **Labyrinthitis / vestibular neuritis**: acute vertigo lasting days, but hearing loss is **variable** – if hearing loss severe, more likely labyrinthitis; check for **fever** and **recent viral illness**.
- **Acoustic neuroma** – progressive (not always sudden) unilateral hearing loss with tinnitus and imbalance; **MRI IAM with contrast** is diagnostic.
- **Cochlear otosclerosis** – bilateral low-frequency loss; **absent stapedius reflex** and CT changes.
- **Autoimmune inner ear disease** – rapidly progressive bilateral hearing loss, often associated with **systemic autoimmune conditions** (lupus, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Cogan syndrome); check **ANA, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP, c-ANCA**.
- **Stroke (AICA/PICA territory)** – acute hearing loss + vertigo + ataxia; **HINTS exam positive**.
- **Ototoxicity** – recent use of aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, aspirin, chemotherapy (cisplatin); **tinnitus often precedes hearing loss**.
- **Trauma** – temporal bone fracture, barotrauma (recent flying/diving); **CT temporal bone** if history suggests.
- **Splitter:** **Fever + normal TM + headache** → meningoencephalitis, not idiopathic SSNHL. Perform LP.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Pure tone audiometry (PTA) – urgent (same day) to confirm ≥30dB loss in ≥3 consecutive frequencies; if not available immediately, use tuning fork (Rinne/Weber) + smartphone hearing test app as screen.
  - Otoscopy to rule out conductive causes (wax, OME, cholesteatoma)
  - Full neurological exam including HINTS if vertigo
  - Vital signs, NEWS2
  - Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia can mimic stroke)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-CCP, ANCA, anti-dsDNA) – if bilateral, young, or recurrent
  - Syphilis serology (TPPA/RPR) – rare but treatable cause
  - Thyroid function (hypothyroidism associated)
  - Coagulation screen (if anticoagulated) – but not routine
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain with IAM and inner ear internal auditory meatus (IAM) and cerebellopontine angle (CPA) sequences – to exclude acoustic neuroma, stroke, MS, meningitis. Urgent if neurological signs present.
  - **Second-line:** CT temporal bone (if MRI contraindicated or if trauma suspected) – poor sensitivity for small tumours.
- **Special tests:**
  - Auditory brainstem response (ABR) – if MRI not possible
  - ECG / echocardiogram / carotid doppler – only if stroke suspected (per stroke protocol)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pure tone audiometry** – if not done, you cannot distinguish true SSNHL from conductive loss or malingering, and you cannot monitor recovery. Also **urgent MRI IAM** within 24-48h if unilateral – missing a schwannoma is a medicolegal error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude stroke (HINTS, NIHSS if any focal sign) – if stroke suspected, activate stroke pathway, do NOT start steroids.
  - Once stroke ruled out, start **high-dose corticosteroids** as soon as possible (within 24h of onset) – oral or intratympanic, per local protocol.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: age >60, vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension, smoking), vertigo, bilateral loss, or if patient cannot manage outpatient steroids.
  - Continue oral steroids for 7-14 days then taper unless contraindicated.
  - Consider intratympanic steroids (dexamethasone injection by ENT) if oral steroids contraindicated or as salvage therapy if no response to oral after 2 weeks.
  - Add **antiviral** (acyclovir/valacyclovir) only if suspected viral cause (Ramsay Hunt syndrome – vesicular rash in ear canal / pinna) – not routine.
  - Add **hyperbaric oxygen** if available and within 2 weeks of onset – limited evidence, but some protocols (consider in severe/profound loss).
  - Audiology follow-up for repeat PTA at 2-4 weeks.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If partial recovery, consider hearing aid or bone conduction device.
  - If severe/profound loss >6 months and no recovery, cochlear implant referral.
  - MRI IAM at 6-12 months if initial MRI normal but hearing loss persists – to detect slow-growing tumour.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: not needed unless poor oral intake or vomiting (then IV 0.9% saline 1-2L/24h)
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess risk (immobility, age) – if admitted, standard enoxaparin unless contraindicated
  - Diet: normal as tolerated; avoid NBM unless for surgery
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; if stable, 6-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent: Prednisolone oral**
  - Dose: 1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) for 7 days, then taper over 7-14 days (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2-3 days). **[check local policy for exact tapering]**
  - Alternatively: dexamethasone 0.1 mg/kg IV (if oral not tolerated) – **[check local policy]**
- **Intratympanic steroid (second-line or salvage):**
  - Dexamethasone injection (4-24 mg/mL) – given weekly for 2-3 doses by ENT
  - Methylprednisolone (40 mg/mL) – alternative
- **Antiviral (if Ramsay Hunt suspected):**
  - Aciclovir: 800 mg PO 5 times daily for 7-10 days; or IV 5-10 mg/kg 8-hourly if severe
  - Valaciclovir: 1 g PO 3 times daily for 7 days
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Do not give corticosteroids if active infection (TB, fungal, uncontrolled diabetes) or if acute stroke not yet excluded.
  - Monitor blood glucose in diabetics – steroids will increase sugar; start insulin sliding scale if required.
  - Avoid NSAIDs while on steroids (increased GI risk).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment for prednisolone; for aciclovir – reduce dose if eGFR <25 mL/min (use dose per BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying steroids** – every hour of delay reduces chance of recovery. Also, giving steroids before excluding stroke/hyperglycaemia/meningitis can be catastrophic.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Steroid side effects: hyperglycaemia, insomnia, mood changes, hypertension, hypokalaemia
  - Falls due to vertigo (especially elderly)
  - Missed diagnosis of acoustic neuroma leading to progressive brainstem compression
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent hearing loss (profound deafness in 30-50% untreated, but 50-65% recover some hearing with steroids)
  - Chronic tinnitus – can impair quality of life
  - Secondary depression / anxiety
sections.Prognosis:
- Spontaneous recovery: **30-65%** (higher if mild loss, younger age, no vertigo, early treatment <7 days)
- With steroids: **50-85%** partial or complete recovery
- Poor prognostic factors: severe/profound loss at onset, vertigo present, older age, diabetes, late presentation (>2 weeks), bilateral loss
- Mortality: essentially zero (unless caused by stroke or meningitis)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform tuning fork tests (Rinne/Weber)** to confirm sensorineural – misdiagnosing conductive loss as SSNHL leads to unnecessary steroids.
- **Start steroids immediately once stroke excluded** – even before audiometry if tuning fork suggests sensorineural and history clear.
- **Refer to ENT urgently** (same day) for intratympatic steroid consideration and MRI planning – delay reduces chance of hearing preservation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not discharge without audiometry** – you need baseline to monitor treatment response and to confirm diagnosis.
- **Do not give steroids if stroke is not ruled out** – especially in elderly with vascular risk factors – can worsen outcomes.
- **Do not forget to check blood glucose before starting high-dose steroids** – risk of DKA/HHS in undiagnosed diabetes.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New/worsening vertigo, headache, fever, or neurological symptoms (suggests stroke or meningitis)
  - Severe pain in or behind ear (may be herpes zoster oticus)
  - Worsening hearing or new tinnitus (progression of underlying cause)
- **Follow-up:**
  - ENT clinic within 1-2 weeks (for repeat audiometry and plan)
  - If intratympanic steroids given, follow ENT for repeat injection schedule
  - If MRI IAM planned, ensure appointment within 2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar) if:**
  - Any focal neurology (stroke)
  - Bilateral sudden hearing loss (suggests autoimmune, meningeal, vascular)
  - Patient unwell with fever, headache, meningism
  - Lack of response to steroids after 2 weeks (consider salvage therapy)
  - Suspected acoustic neuroma (need urgent MRI)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - GCS <13
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, sixth nerve palsy, hypertension with bradycardia)
  - Seizures or status epilepticus
  - Meningococcal rash or rapid deterioration

rawMarkdown:
## Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss
**One-liner:** A rapid (<72h) hearing loss of cochlear/retrocochlear origin; an ENT emergency where early steroids salvage hearing, but mimic stroke must be excluded first.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Vertebrobasilar stroke** – the single finding that says "this is not SSNHL": **any neurological symptom** (vertigo with nystagmus, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria, hemiparesis, unexplained hiccups) or **HINTS positive** (head impulse normal, nystagmus direction-changing, test of skew vertical). Do not start steroids until CNS cause ruled out.
- **Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)** – not immediately fatal, but missed diagnosis leads to permanent hearing loss and brainstem compression; red flag: **unilateral hearing loss with ipsilateral facial numbness, tinnitus, or imbalance** – needs MRI IAM/CPA.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** – hearing loss can be the sole presentation of temporal bone infection; findings: **fever, neck stiffness, headache, altered mental status** – perform urgent LP if suspected.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset (<72h) of hearing loss in one ear (rarely bilateral)
  - Often noticed on awakening or while using phone
  - Tinnitus (ringing/buzzing) in affected ear – >80% of patients
  - Aural fullness (like "ear needs popping") – may mimic OME
  - Vertigo/dizziness **only in 30–40%** – if present, strongly suspect stroke or labyrinthitis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Otoscopy:** normal ear canal and tympanic membrane (TM) – no wax, no fluid, no perforation. If TM abnormal → conductive loss, not SSNHL.
  - **Rinne test**: air conduction > bone conduction (Rinne positive) – confirms sensorineural component
  - **Weber test**: lateralizes to **better ear** (if unilateral loss)
  - **Neurological exam**: full cranial nerves, cerebellar tests, gait – **must be normal** to rule out stroke
  - **Vestibular exam (if dizzy)**: head impulse test, nystagmus, test of skew – HINTS exam to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely** → **Idiopathic SSNHL** (70-90%): no identifiable cause after standard workup; diagnosis of exclusion.
- **Meniere’s disease**: episodic vertigo + fluctuating hearing + fullness + tinnitus; differentiate by **episodic vertigo lasting hours**, not just at onset.
- **Labyrinthitis / vestibular neuritis**: acute vertigo lasting days, but hearing loss is **variable** – if hearing loss severe, more likely labyrinthitis; check for **fever** and **recent viral illness**.
- **Acoustic neuroma** – progressive (not always sudden) unilateral hearing loss with tinnitus and imbalance; **MRI IAM with contrast** is diagnostic.
- **Cochlear otosclerosis** – bilateral low-frequency loss; **absent stapedius reflex** and CT changes.
- **Autoimmune inner ear disease** – rapidly progressive bilateral hearing loss, often associated with **systemic autoimmune conditions** (lupus, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Cogan syndrome); check **ANA, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP, c-ANCA**.
- **Stroke (AICA/PICA territory)** – acute hearing loss + vertigo + ataxia; **HINTS exam positive**.
- **Ototoxicity** – recent use of aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, aspirin, chemotherapy (cisplatin); **tinnitus often precedes hearing loss**.
- **Trauma** – temporal bone fracture, barotrauma (recent flying/diving); **CT temporal bone** if history suggests.
- **Splitter:** **Fever + normal TM + headache** → meningoencephalitis, not idiopathic SSNHL. Perform LP.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Pure tone audiometry (PTA) – urgent (same day) to confirm ≥30dB loss in ≥3 consecutive frequencies; if not available immediately, use tuning fork (Rinne/Weber) + smartphone hearing test app as screen.
  - Otoscopy to rule out conductive causes (wax, OME, cholesteatoma)
  - Full neurological exam including HINTS if vertigo
  - Vital signs, NEWS2
  - Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia can mimic stroke)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-CCP, ANCA, anti-dsDNA) – if bilateral, young, or recurrent
  - Syphilis serology (TPPA/RPR) – rare but treatable cause
  - Thyroid function (hypothyroidism associated)
  - Coagulation screen (if anticoagulated) – but not routine
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain with IAM and inner ear internal auditory meatus (IAM) and cerebellopontine angle (CPA) sequences – to exclude acoustic neuroma, stroke, MS, meningitis. Urgent if neurological signs present.
  - **Second-line:** CT temporal bone (if MRI contraindicated or if trauma suspected) – poor sensitivity for small tumours.
- **Special tests:**
  - Auditory brainstem response (ABR) – if MRI not possible
  - ECG / echocardiogram / carotid doppler – only if stroke suspected (per stroke protocol)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pure tone audiometry** – if not done, you cannot distinguish true SSNHL from conductive loss or malingering, and you cannot monitor recovery. Also **urgent MRI IAM** within 24-48h if unilateral – missing a schwannoma is a medicolegal error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude stroke (HINTS, NIHSS if any focal sign) – if stroke suspected, activate stroke pathway, do NOT start steroids.
  - Once stroke ruled out, start **high-dose corticosteroids** as soon as possible (within 24h of onset) – oral or intratympanic, per local protocol.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: age >60, vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension, smoking), vertigo, bilateral loss, or if patient cannot manage outpatient steroids.
  - Continue oral steroids for 7-14 days then taper unless contraindicated.
  - Consider intratympanic steroids (dexamethasone injection by ENT) if oral steroids contraindicated or as salvage therapy if no response to oral after 2 weeks.
  - Add **antiviral** (acyclovir/valacyclovir) only if suspected viral cause (Ramsay Hunt syndrome – vesicular rash in ear canal / pinna) – not routine.
  - Add **hyperbaric oxygen** if available and within 2 weeks of onset – limited evidence, but some protocols (consider in severe/profound loss).
  - Audiology follow-up for repeat PTA at 2-4 weeks.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If partial recovery, consider hearing aid or bone conduction device.
  - If severe/profound loss >6 months and no recovery, cochlear implant referral.
  - MRI IAM at 6-12 months if initial MRI normal but hearing loss persists – to detect slow-growing tumour.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: not needed unless poor oral intake or vomiting (then IV 0.9% saline 1-2L/24h)
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess risk (immobility, age) – if admitted, standard enoxaparin unless contraindicated
  - Diet: normal as tolerated; avoid NBM unless for surgery
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; if stable, 6-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent: Prednisolone oral**
  - Dose: 1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) for 7 days, then taper over 7-14 days (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2-3 days). **[check local policy for exact tapering]**
  - Alternatively: dexamethasone 0.1 mg/kg IV (if oral not tolerated) – **[check local policy]**
- **Intratympanic steroid (second-line or salvage):**
  - Dexamethasone injection (4-24 mg/mL) – given weekly for 2-3 doses by ENT
  - Methylprednisolone (40 mg/mL) – alternative
- **Antiviral (if Ramsay Hunt suspected):**
  - Aciclovir: 800 mg PO 5 times daily for 7-10 days; or IV 5-10 mg/kg 8-hourly if severe
  - Valaciclovir: 1 g PO 3 times daily for 7 days
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Do not give corticosteroids if active infection (TB, fungal, uncontrolled diabetes) or if acute stroke not yet excluded.
  - Monitor blood glucose in diabetics – steroids will increase sugar; start insulin sliding scale if required.
  - Avoid NSAIDs while on steroids (increased GI risk).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment for prednisolone; for aciclovir – reduce dose if eGFR <25 mL/min (use dose per BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying steroids** – every hour of delay reduces chance of recovery. Also, giving steroids before excluding stroke/hyperglycaemia/meningitis can be catastrophic.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Steroid side effects: hyperglycaemia, insomnia, mood changes, hypertension, hypokalaemia
  - Falls due to vertigo (especially elderly)
  - Missed diagnosis of acoustic neuroma leading to progressive brainstem compression
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent hearing loss (profound deafness in 30-50% untreated, but 50-65% recover some hearing with steroids)
  - Chronic tinnitus – can impair quality of life
  - Secondary depression / anxiety

### Prognosis
- Spontaneous recovery: **30-65%** (higher if mild loss, younger age, no vertigo, early treatment <7 days)
- With steroids: **50-85%** partial or complete recovery
- Poor prognostic factors: severe/profound loss at onset, vertigo present, older age, diabetes, late presentation (>2 weeks), bilateral loss
- Mortality: essentially zero (unless caused by stroke or meningitis)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform tuning fork tests (Rinne/Weber)** to confirm sensorineural – misdiagnosing conductive loss as SSNHL leads to unnecessary steroids.
- **Start steroids immediately once stroke excluded** – even before audiometry if tuning fork suggests sensorineural and history clear.
- **Refer to ENT urgently** (same day) for intratympatic steroid consideration and MRI planning – delay reduces chance of hearing preservation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not discharge without audiometry** – you need baseline to monitor treatment response and to confirm diagnosis.
- **Do not give steroids if stroke is not ruled out** – especially in elderly with vascular risk factors – can worsen outcomes.
- **Do not forget to check blood glucose before starting high-dose steroids** – risk of DKA/HHS in undiagnosed diabetes.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New/worsening vertigo, headache, fever, or neurological symptoms (suggests stroke or meningitis)
  - Severe pain in or behind ear (may be herpes zoster oticus)
  - Worsening hearing or new tinnitus (progression of underlying cause)
- **Follow-up:**
  - ENT clinic within 1-2 weeks (for repeat audiometry and plan)
  - If intratympanic steroids given, follow ENT for repeat injection schedule
  - If MRI IAM planned, ensure appointment within 2 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar) if:**
  - Any focal neurology (stroke)
  - Bilateral sudden hearing loss (suggests autoimmune, meningeal, vascular)
  - Patient unwell with fever, headache, meningism
  - Lack of response to steroids after 2 weeks (consider salvage therapy)
  - Suspected acoustic neuroma (need urgent MRI)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - GCS <13
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, sixth nerve palsy, hypertension with bradycardia)
  - Seizures or status epilepticus
  - Meningococcal rash or rapid deterioration
